data.title: Sandisk: I'm Not Chasing This Cycle - Not Here
data.description:
Sandisk (SNDK) is clearly in an extraordinary pricing-led acceleration phase with rapidly expanding margins, as demand stays strong and supply remains constrained. However, with volume gains appearing only incremental in the near future, the only road ahead to continued earnings growth is through further pricing acceleration beyond Q3 FY2026.

There is less doubt now that Q3 FY2026 numbers will come out great and support the upward trajectory in terms of operational numbers. But the forward regime is more likely to be near the steepest part of the pricing curve, making further price increases likely incremental at best for the next 3-4 quarters - not a continuation of the vertical lift in prices we have seen in the past couple of quarters and likely in Q3 (already guided, implied and digested). Sandisk is moving toward smoother multi-year contract structures with customers, which support a more visible, predictable price regime ahead - but less likely to allow for near vertical rises ahead.

That plateau base case is well protected from major declines though, by structural factors today like hyperscaler capex, supply discipline and long terms contracts. So, we are not likely talking about a peak followed by a deep trough. But, if EBITDA plateaus beyond Q3 as I expect, or even grows modestly, with more linear gains on the pricing curve (a harder ask from here) alone, the multiple may start digesting the EBITDA strength, leaving little room for the shares to grow significantly further.

The overall risk reward is not overwhelmingly signaling a Buy at these levels. The duration of the cycle is strengthened, but cyclicality is not eliminated - giving valuations reasons to cool and allow earnings to grow into them. That is a Hold call for Sandisk today - chasing here is not as obvious as the super strong fundamental trajectory indicates.

Valuation Upside Constrained

At ~13.5x EV to forward EBITDA, Sandisk's valuations are near peak territory for memory cycle plays. These are valuations not assigned to a more diversified memory player like Micron, with exposure to both DRAM and NAND. Sandisk's tighter pure play leverage to NAND pricing and strong pricing momentum today may still account for the gap to an extent. Plus Sandisk's surge at the moment could be in a slightly different cycle compared to Micron, but a look at Micron's historical valuation cycles also do not show a double digit EV to EBITDA multiple, even on a trailing EBITDA basis. There are some jumps in the valuation charts, but that is during EBITDA troughs as markets project forward (not a rally zone for the share, but valuations staying relatively elevated in anticipation of an imminent recovery cycle).

That double digit EV to EBITDA ceiling also is inclusive of the most recent surge in EBITDA due to an unprecedented era of AI-supported and hyperscaler capex driven demand. If we look at prior stable cycles before this breakout, two things become visually clearer. One, valuations actually peak in troughs and already start compressing before an EBITDA up cycle. In fact peak EBITDA often means lowest valuations. And valuations start picking up in anticipation for the next cycle as EBITDA starts contracting. The second thing that is clearly seen is that peak valuations of ~10-12x are seldom sustained.

This time around, the memory cycle dynamics have already flipped in acknowledgement of an AI demand super cycle. We are seeing up-cycle EBITDA conditions and a valuation expansion at the same time. Given that, and the fact that Sandisk's valuations are far ahead of Micron even in the same extended cycle optimism regime, it makes it harder to justify a case for further valuation expansion. Either EBITDA plateaus or slows and valuations stay where they are, or they could cool and offset earnings growth from here on - that is the more likely path to expect.

The EBITDA Plateau Base Case

Since valuation expansion looks tougher, the path to a high conviction Buy requires an expanding EBITDA path ahead at a decent pace too - otherwise the returns could be far slower and risks become more pronounced (i.e. a less asymmetrical setup for the stock).

From a demand perspective, the Q2 FY2026 call clarified that supply is the bottleneck -

Given the strength of the market, we were unable to fulfill demand for our customers this quarter. We're evolving how we define strategic engagement, prioritizing customers with multiyear supply frameworks and share planning commitments over transactional short-term demand signals. We continue to be prudent and are not changing our capital spending plans, which support mid- to high-teens bit growth through the BiCS8 transition.

And the bottleneck is severe enough to provide multi-year visibility in many cases. That shows demand strength is not only visible, but a near term trough is not the most worrying factor for Sandisk's Buy thesis. The volume shipment has not kept pace with the demand and bits are growing at low single digits sequentially as of Q2. Supply is not likely to ease meaningfully in the near term (no increase in capital spending and guided net bit effect of mid to high teens growth over time, mainly through technology transitions (BiCS8).

The revenue in Q2 was ~31% higher sequentially, and in the absence of commensurate volume growth, again establishes a pricing led revenue jump over volumes. Now, a further step change in Q3 is already embedded in expectations. Volumes are not likely to grow aggressively (looking at the low single digit bit growth levels exiting Q2). So the revenue jump from ~$3b to a guided midpoint of ~$4.4b, will almost entirely be pricing led again. The pricing-led gains thesis is further validated by the increased non-GAAP gross margin guidance of 65-67% (up from ~51.1% in Q2) in Q3. Beyond Q3, if pricing does not increase further, a mid to high teens bit growth is the best case translated growth in revenue as well and that too is a structural annual expectation, not sequential.

The Q3 guidance also means a steep EBITDA jump again, after margins have already expanded significantly from 29.9% in Q1 to ~51.1% in Q2. To arrive at a rough indicator of operating expenses, I use the gross profit margin of ~51.1% in Q2 (means ~$1.53b gross profits), and the reported EBITDA of ~$1.1b - the difference between the two is ~$0.45b, which is the approximated fixed costs. Extrapolating the same framework to Q3 and expecting similar fixed costs, we have a gross profit of ~$3b implied from the revenue guidance of ~$4.4b and a 65-67% gross margin - and taking out the Q2-level fixed costs from that profit, we have an EBITDA of ~$2.5b. That is a steep step jump from Q2, and a continuation of the recent surge from the $200-300m levels.

From Q3, the road becomes more challenging for Sandisk for further material EBITDA gains. There are structural reasons to believe why pricing has limited room to run beyond Q3, and hence a plateau (if not normalization) is the most likely base case to expect. The higher base for prices and the extent of the recent surge are factors, but cannot be dependable thesis anchors. However, further supply tightness is neither indicated, nor likely - in fact the technology boost to bit (BiCS8 transition) and volumes is a long term trend that points to slightly expanding supply over time. And if volumes stay stable and not shrink further, pricing stability appears to be the best case outcome, not a further surge of significant magnitudes.

While supply tightness related pricing push is not indicated, the demand side could theoretically get even more bullish. But there too, the bullish looks to be supportive of multi-quarter stability rather than incrementally more positive. Sandisk has already indicated a shift toward a smoother multi-year supply contract framework. That is great for the cyclicality fears and could be valuation-supportive (already at highs at a time when EBITDA is expanding exponentially), but limits scope for step jumps in pricing. Incremental adjustments and stability is still in play - but aggressive surges look less probable. All in all, without ruling out further EBITDA expansion, I feel it is best to expect some slowing down and plateauing of EBITDA trends beyond Q3. Which means the annualized EBITDA expectations ahead should be in the range of $10b+, and the question should shift to a continuation of that baseline (less cyclicality) rather than further step jump expansion.

The Lack of Asymmetry

Assuming a lack of significant price elasticity beyond Q3, the adjusted forward EV to EBITDA is closer to the ~10x mark - not ~13x as the markets are factoring in today based on forward EBITDA expectations that are lower than my base case extrapolation. Unfortunately, that relative conservatism in the markets, does not open up a Buy window for Sandisk - and that is because even at ~10x, there is low room for convincing multiple expansion. That we have established studying Micron across cycles. Ideally, multiples should have been contracting already in any other regular cycle as EBITDA surges, in anticipation of a peak. This time, markets are holding the premium because of a stronger and longer cycle with lower troughs. That is understandable. But further expansion from here is a tall ask.

The bullish scenario of multiples holding long enough at say ~13x and an EBITDA run rate of ~$10b, gives a path to another 30% gain in share prices from current levels. Let's peg that upside even further up at 50%, assuming EBITDA grows through continued pricing surge beyond Q3 and lands up at ~$11.5b annually. Even then, the risk-reward asymmetry is not as clean as it was in 2025. This is because the bear case today is that multiples compress toward ~7x (still quite strong compared to historical memory cycles and for memory companies), and EBITDA run rates normalize toward ~$8b. This is not a very strong bearish case, and requires only a minor uplift in operating costs (I assume to be at Q2 levels with no increment), or some pricing, volume, demand normalization. This conservative bearish scenario alone could see a 45% correction in share prices.

Overall, Sandisk is best seen as a Hold, acknowledging the AI-led demand strength that has altered its cyclicality. This is already acknowledged to a large extent in valuations today. Those who have missed out in 2026, chasing Sandisk now is not likely to give a repeat of the upside seen in the past 3-4 months.

data.title: Comcast: Broadband Customer Base In Focus Ahead Of Q1
data.description:
Comcast (CMCSA) is one name that I view as interesting at current trading levels. In my 2026 market outlook article, which was published in late 2025, I noted that a key theme for this year would be selectivity over momentum.

The ongoing war in Iran, as well as growing fears of AI and its implications for businesses, have turned some of my predictions into fruition. Keeping with that theme, I believe CMCSA has the makings to be of those ‘selective’ additions to investor portfolios.

Shares in the cable-and-entertainment company currently trade at the back-end of its 52-week range at what I view to be a discounted trading multiple. In addition, shares come attached with a dividend yield north of 4.5%.

The company will be releasing its Q1 later in April, and ahead of that release, I believe shares are worth holding onto. For those seeking new positioning, however, I believe its worth waiting for more stabilization in the broadband category before initiating. And that is where my focus will be for the upcoming quarter.

CMCSA Stock Key Metrics

Most of the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) analyst community is bullish on CMCSA, and I believe this is for good reason.

For one, the stock trades at an attractive valuation, in my view. Its forward multiple to earnings, for example, stands at about 9.5x. That’s below both the peer set as well as to CMCSA’s own historical averages.

The valuation is supported further by its enterprise value, which is just 5x its forward EBTIDA and 1.6x sales. That’s also a sizeable discount relative to peers and to the sector.

The favorable valuation score is working in its favor on the quants, but that is where the good news seems to end.

CMCSA is viewed more pessimistically in terms of growth and momentum. Within the former, the continuing loss of domestic broadband customers is one concern, and I believe this will ultimately need to be addressed before investors can turn more positive on the forward outlook.

What Is The Outlook For CMCSA Stock?

From what I can glean from the most recent Q4 print, 2026 is looking to be a transition year for CMCSA.

In his remarks, CEO Brian Roberts acknowledged that broadband is under real pressure. But instead of defending the status quo mainstay of the business, plans were outlined to overhaul the go-to-market model.

This includes simpler pricing, a 5-year price guarantee, heavier wireless bundling, and major customer experience investments. All of these are expected to first stop the bleeding in broadband and then return the segment to growth.

This pivot comes with near-term EBITDA pressure, which the management team was upfront about. The second half of 2026, however, should look materially different as free wireless lines convert to paying customers and most of the base migrates to higher-value gig tiers.

On a more forward looking basis, it’s promising that CMCSA isn’t dependent entirely on broadband and that there are other aspects to its outlook.

Wireless, for example, grew 18%, Peacock revenue jumped 23%, and Theme Parks posted 22% growth.

I view these categories as meaningful growth drivers helping offset cable pressure, and looking ahead, I believe the company will likely continue leaning into its diversification while it resets its core.

What To Watch When CMCSA Reports Q1 Results?

When CMCSA reports Q1, I’ll be watching for evidence that the broadband reset is working.

In setting guidance, CMCSA was very transparent that the first half of 2026 will carry incremental EBITDA pressure as the company pushes through simplified pricing, free wireless line promotions, and heavier customer experience investments.

So the headline numbers may still look messy. Broadband ARPU is expected to remain under pressure. Subscriber losses may not yet meaningfully improve. And Connectivity & Platforms EBITDA could continue to feel the weight of these investments.

What would matter more in my lens is the underlying trend data.

Are voluntary churn rates continuing to improve? Is adoption of the 5-year price guarantee growing? Are more customers moving into gig-plus speed tiers? Is wireless attachment continuing at a healthy pace?

These are the metrics management clearly signaled as leading indicators that the strategy is gaining traction.

Investors should also watch wireless net adds and commentary around how many customers are taking free lines, since those are expected to convert into paying relationships in the back half of the year.

Finally, listen closely for commentary around customer experience metrics like NPS and trouble calls. CMCSA repeatedly emphasized these as critical to stabilizing broadband performance before growth can return.

Is CMCSA Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At current trading levels, I view CMCSA as a hold with upside, particularly for income-oriented investors.

In my view, the stock is currently not being rewarded for what the business could look like in the second half of 2026 and beyond. Instead, it is being priced for what broadband looks like today. And that is pressured, competitive, and shrinking.

That disconnect is where I think the opportunity sits, but it is also why I’m not ready to call this an outright ‘buy’ just yet.

CMCSA has made it clear that the first half of the year will look noisy. EBITDA will feel the weight of investments. Broadband metrics may not immediately improve. And sentiment could remain muted as those headline numbers roll in.

But beneath that, I believe there is a deliberate strategy underway to stabilize the core while leaning into faster-growing businesses like wireless, Peacock, and Theme Parks.

If the early indicators begin to show that churn is improving, gig-tier adoption is rising, and wireless attachment continues to build, the narrative around this stock can change fairly quickly.

In the meantime, investors are being paid to wait with a dividend yield north of 4.5%. For current holders, I believe CMCSA is a name worth sticking with. For new investors, however, I would wait for clearer signs that the broadband reset is gaining traction before stepping in.

data.title: Capstone Copper: Why The Market May Be Short-Sighted
data.description:
Notice: All Dollar amounts are in Canadian dollars, except when otherwise stated

One of the things that I do when a market or sector is outperforming is outlining the companies in that sector, and then finding the one that doesn't fit - i.e., in this case, the one that doesn't outperform.

If I find such a business, I then go in and look at the reasons why the company is underperforming. If I then consider these reasons to be short-sighted and poor, I may end up investing in the business.

For some time now, copper mining (well, mining in general and metals, but copper as well) has had a very good time on the market. Many of the leading copper companies, or companies that recycle it, which I also cover, have been doing very well for themselves. One company, meanwhile, that hasn't done well for itself despite surges in the sector, is Capstone Copper (CS:CA) (CSCCF).

I quite often review Canadian companies, and I intend to continue doing so. In this case, Capstone Copper is a relatively big company, with an $8.5B market cap. Its rating is BB-, meaning it does not qualify as an IG-rated company, and it also does not carry a dividend. However, if you follow my work, you'll know that neither of these fundamentals is a 100% requirement for me to invest in a business. I, in fact, invest in businesses that don't have these things from time to time.

What matters to me is realistic value and fundamentals, and how those are expected to change. And I believe, in terms of commodities here, there is a case to be made for significant upside.

In this article, I will look at the company's fundamentals, see what could cause the company to rise (as well as the reasons for its recent underperformance), and why such a rise may indeed be likely (or why the market in some cases, is undervaluing the company).

Capstone Copper - Exhibiting typical commodity volatility

Capstone Copper dropped more than 20% in the matter of a month, from close to $16/share to less than $10/share. Why did this happen, first of all?

A combination of reasons can be put forward. The company released results and guidance, neither of which impressed the market all that much. The production target fell to about 230k tonnes, and profits were expected to be lower due to operational cost increases. Furthermore, the company reported lower ore grades at one of its assets, and is experiencing strike impacts at another asset. It's not strange that the market, at this particular time, is not exactly a "fan" of Capstone.

But is this enough to drive the company to the levels we're seeing today? As I am writing, we have a share price of about $11 for CS (the Canadian ticker). What does this means.

The company's BB- credit rating would typically imply subpar fundamentals and potential issues. I argue that this is not necessarily the case. The company is experiencing a significant ramp-up in production, potential cost efficiencies, and organic growth potential, which also echoes throughout its forecast. Its 2025 production was at record levels. The company reported more than 184k tonnes.

Furthermore, despite lower ore grades from one asset, I argue following research into the business, that the company has a very clear pathway and investment road to becoming a mid-tier copper producer (one of the best) at 2027-2028 at the latest.

How do I justify this view?

The company has high historical costs and inefficiencies for mining. These are improved. Capstone is currently forecasting as low as $2.2/lb, and even the lower-grade ore only affects this so much. However, we must expect the operational expectations, insofar as goes efficiency and production costs or qualities to remain fluid. What also acts as a nice buffer or quality indicator for the company is its fundamentals. Despite BB-, Capstone has a leverage of below 3.5x to EBITDA - in fact, S&P Global expects it to remain below 2x, even as the company grows. Recent refinancing of its debt adds to the positivity here, because the company is now left with over $1B in available liquidity, which means that it can fund its growth projects without even needing to excessively tap equity.

The company is essentially a play on 4 operations in Copper, all attractively positioned in the Americas. We have Mantoverde in Chile, with a 70% ownership; we have Mantos Blancos, also in Chile, and at a 100% ownership; we have the Pinto Valley in AZ, where there are already operations, but where Capstone also owns 100%, and we have the latest Cozamin asset in Mexico, owned at 100%. As you can see, the company owns most of its assets already, and the company has assets coming online as well.

The combination of asset optimization (MV, above all), and new assets being brought online means that I view the credit ratings as lagging behind quite a bit. The BB-, in my view, gives needless focus and excessive lack of expectation for the company's current operations, while not taking into consideration its expansion. It does not account, in my view, properly for the company's ramp-up as well as the improved cost profile.

The same, I believe, is true for the company's current valuation.

When looking at a commodity, what I look at is supply and demand. When it comes to the copper demand, it is to me abundantly clear that there is a significant shortcoming in supply. This is not just related to EV and AI - it's also related to infrastructure. There is a significant and structural deficit coming (and in some ways, already here) in global copper production relative to demand. Current estimates put the shortfall somewhere at 8 million tonnes by 2030 - this means mining needs to increase, and the miners that do are likely to see significant upside. There are more complex, sector-specific issues with copper, such as a generally lower grade of copper being mined, which makes increasing production hard, and a very long processing time from exploring or scoping a copper asset to the actual start of production. It's a very labour-intensive process.

I therefore believe the market is underestimating what Capstone Copper is doing, and what it will be able to do with what it has going forward, even if the cost decreases do not go as far as some expect them to.

Capstone Copper is a profitable operator. It consistently generates positive net income, operating cash flow and mostly generates positive FCF. Some of its fundamentals are currently being muddled by the operational ramp-up. There's no doubt that by investing here, you're betting on "getting in" before things ramp up.

However, the beauty here with this investment is this.

I consider it materially unlikely that Capstone Copper at any near-term point in time goes bankrupt or faces significant operational or financial issues. If you agree that this is the case, then, conversely, the company's upside is a question of time, or of market fundamentals and earnings. The company's short-term challenges, including labor disputes and the like, are what I would consider immaterial to the larger picture. We have a company that is growing very rapidly, very strongly, and we have a multi-year global positive case for copper, with a structural deficit even if all of the expected capacity comes online.

I believe rising operational costs for Capstone are likely to be mitigated by the lower-cost production areas in the Americas. The notion that the lower ore grade suggests that the Manto Blancos asset is somehow worth a lot less only exhibits a lack of understanding for copper mining - the lower grade is only a one-year thing, and it's a predictable part of how copper mines work, as you can read about here.

The long-term bullish case remains a strong one because the demand for copper is strong as a whole - we're talking a mix of EV, renewables, utilities, we're talking low-cost expansions, we're talking assets in comparatively safe geographies as opposed to competitors which operate in much higher-risk geographies.

While the company doesn't have the strongest balance sheet I have seen, it's nonetheless one that, for the purpose of what the company is doing, is strong enough to sustain it until capacity is online and production ramps up. Even with volatility from costs and from operational issues, I forecast at least $1.2-$1.3/share once the growth projects for the company come online. Consider for a moment that the 2025A results were about $0.3/share, and that should give you an indication of what the company could be worth.

Here's my view on this.

Capstone Copper valuation - hard to forecast, hard to value

That the company is tricky to forecast, there is no doubt. The lack of a dividend is a fundamental problem that needs to be discounted for, as is the volatility in the business.

The sector average for the copper industry is about 20x P/E. This is also the company's historical average, lending some credence to the multiple. A bearish scenario for the company would include lower expected ore prices, higher power prices, and higher feedstock prices. Some have the bearish PT at about $7-$9/share for the company, so about 20-25% lower than the current share price. Analysts would disagree. The low-end share price for Capstone is at above $12/share with a high end of $20/share, and an average of $16.3/share, implying an upside of at least 47% at a NAV of 0.81x.

The problem that I see with the bearish estimates and expectations is that these seem to be based on short-term problems. I find very few long-term bearish variables, with the exception of structural demand decline in China. Even in the event of this, I would maintain that there's a structural deficit for copper globally.

Even at a sector average of 20x P/E, the current ramp-up expectations for 2027, with an EPS of about $1.4/share, imply a share price of close to $30/share. That's an upside of 157%, or about 72% per year.

I consider the $1.4/share estimate to be not unrealistic because I see the Mantoverde optimization as likely to be executed properly. There is very little cause to believe that copper prices are significantly decreasing - averages suggest about $10,000 per tonne in 2027E, and because of the company's low cost structure, they ae set to take advantage of this. Even if we don't expect the company to, but instead work at 2025 numbers for the 2027E production, with a conservative estimate of 260k tonnes (the company estimates 300,000 by 2028-2029) would come to revenues of about $2.8B., and with a 45%+ EBITDA margin that would come to almost $1.3B of EBITDA in 2027 - that's not only up from 2024, but up from 2025 as well. We use EBITDA to calculate the company because it works to isolate profitability from CapEx and debt, as well as taxes typical of the industry.

It's my view that the company is being viewed, as of the share price decline, mostly for the short term. Its valuation trends are going in the opposite direction of the earnings trends - and this is something I actively look for. 2025 was a transformation year, which means that the EPS is not indicative of the long-term. MVO will add 45,000 tonnes per day by the end of 2026. The production growth now achieved will likely pay good dividends (not as in actual dividends, I don't think the company will ever have this) in 2026, and again in 2027.

As a higher-risk producer I would put the company's EBITDA multiple at the lower rather than the higher end of the spectrum. But I would still put it at more than 50% that of the current share price.

Based on my low-end expectation of about $1.2B in EBITDA, this would come to an implied EV of about $12B - and unless we see more debt, that comes to an equity value of at least $10B. Using this based on the company's 764M SO, this comes to a target of at least $13.8/share - and again, this is my PT, and it's on the very low end. I would estimate that the fair value, in a bullish copper scenario, could be easily as high as $20/share, based on allowing a 12-14x EBITDA multiple and more optimistic assumptions for leverage and market pricing.

I therefore put Capstone copper at a conservative PT of $13.5/share, with a fair value up to about $20/share in a positive scenario. That makes the company a "BUY" for me here.

Thesis

I believe the recent moves against Capstone Copper are based on short-term bearish assumptions that have little grounding in fact and the underlying bullish case for copper. It's different valuation trends, therefore, represents an investment opportunity in the commodities sector. Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a $13.5/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized, which the company is doing.

I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "BUY" rating. It may be somewhat speculative, but it's not where I would consider there to be significant danger to investors.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

This company is overall qualitative.This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.This company pays a well-covered dividend. This company is currently cheap.This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a price target of $13.5/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of April 2026.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Commodity Idea investment competition, which runs through May 31. With cash prizes, this competition-open to all analysts-is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Exxon Mobil: War Effect On Earnings
data.description:
Exxon Mobil (XOM) has posted their regular filing of material items that affect earnings along with information on the effects of the war on earnings. The Middle East turns out to have about 20% of Exxon Mobil's production but far less earnings impact. This is going to update the last article as part of a continuing series of what has changed because material changes are likely to be a fact of life until the Middle East settles down.

Nearly everyone has focused on the higher oil prices. But the recent announcement mentioned that production will be impacted by about 6% (compared to the fourth quarter) due to interruptions beginning in March. That has implications going forward until the situation gets fixed because that missing production will be missing at the beginning of the second quarter. It could therefore have a greater impact on the second quarter report than the first quarter report. However, that assumption can change once it is calculated what it takes to restore production and whether that production can be restored in stages or it will happen all at one time. This may also impact world prices because Exxon Mobil is unlikely to be the only major company with disrupted operations.

Timing

Given that prices were rising, there are timing issues related to the "mark to market" of contracts (this process happens at quarter end). But there is no change to the LIFO inventory accounting until the transaction closes. The contracts lose value as the commodity prices rise. But they have also locked in a profit for the transaction. Therefore that "mark to market" is a sort of "non-cash mirage" that disappears when the transaction is finally enacted.

Energy Products operations were affected the most by this timing, with a potential effect of possibly a negative $4 billion to earnings. Far below that is the effect on upstream earnings.

Upstream Summary

The roughly 6% production decline and related effects are shown below:

What is important about this is that right now we have a ceasefire. Therefore, the chances of additional damage are diminished if the ceasefire holds. Unfortunately, that appears to be a big "if" when I am writing this.

Nonetheless, the company was able to point towards things like the startup of the Golden Pass project in the United States that would help offset these declines noted. It is one of the advantages of being geographically diversified.

As the previous article noted, this is a growth and income play. That means there are constant additions of profit possibilities each quarter as various expansion projects come online. That also likely means an additional production boost when repairs are complete on the Middle East assets at some point in the future. But for the time being, production comparisons are likely to at least begin the year by being negative compared to the previous year.

Profit Effect

The big note is that the upstream profit effect is less than the amount of production lost due to damage from the Iran situation.

I borrowed this from a recent Murphy Oil (MUR) presentation on Production Sharing Agreements.

Since we are talking about the Middle East rather than Vietnam, these agreements in the Middle East can be and often are structured differently. But the idea of "risk compared to reward" is the main theme that never changes over the years despite how an agreement is structured.

Basically, the Middle Eastern country affected by the Exxon Mobil agreement has got a profit-sharing arrangement of some sort that allows for sufficient profitability. The less perceived risk at the time of the agreement means that the government will likely have a larger share of the profits.

But the key is for the company to make an adequate profit over time. Now, as to whether the current situation changes the risk calculation is a point for debate until we know for sure all the relevant details. Similarly, how the damages are repaired and the cost of the damage recovery are likely to be revealed by each company over the coming quarters.

One of the things investors are likely to find out is who calculated or evaluated the risk of the Middle East operations the best in terms of cost recovery. Even though the major idea shown above is the same, many of these agreements are negotiated one company at a time.

Summary

Exxon Mobil appears to be diversified enough to avoid the worst effects of the Iranian situation while benefitting from higher commodity prices. I do expect an overall positive effect simply because oil prices rose so much that it would appear to overcome the profit effects of less production and associated damages that need to be repaired.

However, there are no guarantees to that because the timing of sales and some normal business considerations have yet to be publicly discussed.

There were some normal things, such as the strengthening of natural gas prices in the first quarter (in the United States) due to La Niña. Now exactly how all these factors weigh in on the first quarter is a bit hard for me to tell.

However, I do think that this idea remains a strong buy in that the Iranian situation is basically nonrecurring. But there will be some longer-term effects, like lower production, that may persist for some months to potentially a year or two. I think that Exxon Mobil is a solid growth and income idea for the foreseeable future as long as management continues to add value through growth projects.

The latest Dallas Federal Reserve Energy Survey shows that most of the industry regards the current situation as nonrecurring and short-term. That general industry attitude could lead to higher commodity prices than many want to think about for a time period longer than they also want to think about.

But in the short term, that is very bullish for energy. Most everything I have seen regards stock prices as insufficient for the energy price run-up. But that could well straighten itself out as more information on the industry situation becomes available. It would especially help if the Iranian situation got settled.

Risks

If the Iranian situation does not settle down to be replaced by something permanent and constructive, then there could well be more production damages (or disruptions) followed by an ever longer period of much higher energy prices.

Depending upon the future of energy prices, the stock price could get so far ahead of itself that it becomes obvious that it is time to take profits. Right now, that does not appear to be a consideration.

On the other hand, after the current situation is replaced with a permanent agreement, there could be a period of overproduction that would definitely represent a time to consider buying some of these companies.

Oil and Gas is a very low-visibility and volatile industry. Therefore, frequent re-evaluations of the investment are to be expected.

data.title: My Dividend Growth Income: March 2026 Update
data.description:
This year just keeps starting stronger with a record month in terms of forward income added. This month was much more heavily influenced by new purchases, as I made a large additional contribution to the account. I also had an all-time record month in dividends collected in a single month at $912.86. Since this series started in July of 2022, I have collected a total of $19,488.04 in dividends.

Background

The initiation of tracking my DGI income on Seeking Alpha can be found here. My dividend income is tracked across all of my portfolios (taxable accounts and IRAs, not 401(k)s). A large portion of the target $100,000 in forward-projected dividends will be produced within retirement accounts and thus not easily accessible during early retirement; however, I will aim to maintain a 33% proportion of dividend income in my taxable account. With this level of dividend income and adhering to the 4% rule on the overall taxable account size, I will be able to reasonably consider a change in career to a more part-time role or pursue other methods of income until I am able to access retirement funds. Meanwhile, my retirement accounts will continue to build and grow until I'm ready to begin taking distributions to fund my retirement.

Forward Income Added

During the month of February, I added a staggering $308.68 in forward income (an increase of 4.69% month/month), now making my total forward income $6,883.46. I received a new record of single-month dividends in March: $912.86. My dividends received per month have a relatively steady floor month-to-month as investments have been made in monthly dividend payers such as Realty Income Corp. (O), Main Street Capital (MAIN), NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI), and Agree Realty (ADC). On top of this base, I had large dividend payouts from standard quarterly payers as well.

My breakdown of income added via new purchases, dividend reinvestments, and dividend rate increases does not include forward income added via reallocation of funds that already existed within my portfolio (i.e., the money from the sale of one security invested into another). I will only be breaking down the effect of "new" capital entering the accounts in this section. Also, I would like to reiterate that increased income and tracking of funds from my recurring 401(k) contributions are not included in these articles, as the fund options available to me do not paint a clear picture of income and distributions.

Another massive month for my portfolio is in the book, with now roughly 9.44% in total forward dividend growth since the beginning of 2026. I am now substantially ahead of my target pace of 20% dividend growth for the calendar year of 2026. Had it not been for the large additional capital contribution fueling new purchases this month, the contributions to my dividend growth across the three factors I listed before would be well distributed, with a slight skewing towards dividend increases. However, I have been fortunate to be able to add more capital to the account than expected, and dividend growth from new purchases is the driving factor as of today. Here are the forward dividends added via new purchases:

  • 33.059 shares of ADC, adding $103.94 in forward income.
  • 23.513 shares of SPYI, adding $147.26 in forward income.
  • 2.779 shares of Microsoft (MSFT), adding $10.12 in forward income.

Dividend reinvestments brought large growth to my portfolio from a record amount of dividends collected. All in all, I reinvested 15 dividends across 14 different positions (MAIN paid out a supplementary dividend this month), with the main driver of dividend growth stemming from SPYI and the Brookfield positions I own:

  • 0.121 shares of Visa (V) reinvested, adding $0.32 in forward income.
  • 1.692 shares of Main Street Capital Corp. reinvested, adding $5.28 in forward income.
  • 0.098 shares of Agree Realty Corp. reinvested, adding $0.31 in forward income.
  • 0.081 shares of Microsoft reinvested, adding $0.29 in forward income.
  • 2.837 shares of SPYI reinvested, adding $17.38 in forward income.
  • 0.389 shares of Realty Income Corp. reinvested, adding $1.26 in forward income.
  • 0.122 shares of Home Depot (HD) reinvested, adding $1.14 in forward income.
  • 0.053 shares of Lockheed Martin (LMT) reinvested, adding $0.17 in forward income.
  • 0.142 shares of Union Pacific (UNP) reinvested, adding $0.78 in forward income.
  • 0.824 shares of Fidelity National Financial (FNF) reinvested, adding $1.71 in forward income.
  • 0.253 shares of Digital Realty Trust (DLR) reinvested, adding $1.23 in forward income.
  • 0.146 shares of Broadcom (AVGO) reinvested, adding $0.38 in forward income.
  • 5.948 shares of Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) reinvested, adding $10.83 in forward income.
  • 2.848 shares of Brookfield Asset Management (BAM) reinvested, adding $5.72 in forward income.

This month I experienced 1 dividend increase across my portfolio. This was a truly disappointing month for dividend increases, as just one tiny increase was experienced; however, this is typical of March in my portfolio due to the dividend announcement scheduling of my positions:

  • O announced a tiny 0.19% increase to the dividend, adding $0.56 in forward income. Realty Income has been a REIT stalwart for decades, and it is hard for me to be too upset at a company that delivers such consistent income and growth. However, the frequent dividend increases are starting to get smaller and smaller; this will be something to monitor in the coming years.

Below you will find a transaction log of all transactions throughout the month. I like to take a look at the forward dividend income growth attributed to each of my defined transaction types. All in all, I logged 36 total transactions, participating in nearly every market day throughout the month.

Forward Income Added Total Income Added 2026 Percentage of Income Added
New Purchases $261.31

$354.94

 59.49%
Dividend Reinvestment $46.81 $123.94 20.77%
Dividend Increase $0.56 $117.76 19.74%
Total $308.68 $596.64 100%

Portfolio Status and Strategy

The first quarter of 2026 has been about as good as I could possibly hope for in my portfolio. There is a healthy contribution from organic growth (dividend increases + dividend reinvestments) even though inorganic growth (new purchases) is driving the largest contributions. As my portfolio is accelerating due to supplementary purchases on top of my standard weekly buys, it will pay dividends later (literally), as future dividend reinvestments and dividend increase announcements from those positions will be even greater in magnitude.

I have continued to make progress towards achieving "ATM" status on my SPYI and ADC positions, an internal target I set for myself to have the combined monthly output of these two positions total up to $200 in dividends. I now stand at roughly $160 per month between them. Once I reach $200, I will start using their distributions to add to higher growth positions either already in my portfolio or as an initiation of a new ticker.

Yield on cost should be one of the most important metrics for any dividend investor to track, so I like to look at my current status after every month. As of the conclusion of March 2026, my portfolio yield on cost sits at 4.83% vs. a current yield of 3.77%.

Symbol Percent of Account Est. annual income
AVGO 11.31% $172.82
V 9.52% $153.61

MSFT 7.95% $142.26

BAM 6.14% $511.27
MAIN 4.76% $510.18

MPLX 3.76% $515.69

VICI 3.20% $379.43
O 3.16% $303.65
HD 3.15% $162.38

SBUX

 1.61% $81.02

BOC 0.73% N/A

Some positions in my portfolio have been getting absolutely hammered, such as Microsoft and Broadcom. I take solace in the fact that these large resets in stock price still leave me up by multiples due to the length of time I have owned these stocks. I plan to continue owning these strong names well into the future, and I am using this time of weakness and turning it into a time of strength by adding to MSFT and other positions that have been negatively affected in recent months by the wider market pullback.

I think times like these just further emphasize why having a long-term investment horizon is so important. There is no telling when this war in the Middle East will end and how large the impacts will be on the global economy, but we do know that the world will continue to move forward through it and after its conclusion.

Summary

Three transformative months for my portfolio have set me up for a perfect 2026. While I do not believe I can keep up the same level of dividend growth in the upcoming quarters as I have in the past quarter, I am still excited to see how much the account grows and how much my dividends pile up.

At the conclusion of March, I was able to grow my dividend income by $308.68, or 4.69%, month over month, to a total of $6,883.46 in annual dividends. So far in 2026, I have increased my total projected forward income by 9.44% in just the first quarter, forcing me to reassess my year-end target to a higher goal.

data.title: Innodata: Not The Cheapest Data Refiner Around, But The Growth Profile Helps
data.description:
Introduction

Innodata Inc. (INOD), a data engineering specialist that has been honing its expertise in this field for over three-and-a-half decades, has proven - over the past year at least - to be one of the more rewarding bets amongst stocks involved in the terrain of data refining. For added context, note that while an ETF that tracks the FactSet Big Data Refiners Index (DAT) has contracted marginally over the last twelve months, INOD (which admittedly has given up a good chunk of its stellar gains since October 2025) has progressed higher by close to 22%.

If you're intrigued by INOD's relatively strong alpha and want to better understand this story and ascertain if it's worth pursuing, keep reading.

Why Should Investors Consider INOD?

Firstly, it must be said that, in an increasingly digitized world, the importance of data and the domain-specific specialists (INOD leverages the skill set of over 10,000+ subject matter experts across finance, healthcare, law, engineering, sciences, etc.) responsible for screening, chiseling, and fine-tuning that data can hardly be underestimated.

You then have the growing proliferation of artificial intelligence (INOD has been building proprietary AI language models since 2016/17, making it come across as a reliable partner not just for big-tech firms that are building advanced AI systems and models, but also enterprises that are looking to deploy these systems), where the burgeoning vector of agentic AI (enterprise AI systems that don't just reason, but act with greater autonomy and independence) is increasingly making its presence felt in enterprise workflows.

Agentic AI looks set to explode at an outstanding pace of 44% (CAGR) over the next decade, with INOD management even addressing this as "the most significant business innovation opportunity since the advent of electricity". Creating agentic AI systems in a lab-controlled environment is one thing; however, to then get these systems integrated and flourish in a real-world, haphazard environment, where task complexities and threats are often ramped up, is another thing altogether, and that's where a lot of agentic AI developers are falling short. INOD, by virtue of its large, complex, and diverse data resources, is better positioned to cater to this growing agentic AI appetite. Supplementing that, INOD also puts in place a pretty rigorous adversarial simulation system that stress tests these agents against a plethora of different scenarios. Put another way, enterprises that rely on INOD for building their agentic AI capabilities are likely to have a more well-rounded and robust offering. Given these capabilities, it is little wonder that INOD is already servicing 5 out of the Mag 7 entities, besides other AI innovation labs.

Investors should also be aware that INOD has also recently thrown its hat into the Federal AI ring, a market that is expected to grow at 15% over the next couple of years alone. INOD, which only launched Innodata Federal in November 2025, is employing a multi-pronged strategy of not just targeting and locking in immediate prime contract revenue opportunities but also getting into strategic partnerships with defense tech companies and defense contractors.

All in all, what this all points to is a business that is nowhere close to witnessing a meaningful drop-off in growth, despite a larger revenue base (by the year). For instance, from the image above, investors can see that over the past 5 years, INOD's revenue growth has exceeded 35% on average, while last year alone, it came in at an impressive pace of 48%. Normally, when you've just grown your revenue base at near-50% levels, you'd expect a meaningful drop-off in the following year, but INOD's topline in FY26 is poised to grow at a slightly higher pace (at ~37%) than its historical average.

Investors can also take heart from INOD's comforting balance sheet, which should support its future growth ambitions. To elaborate, this is a company that has no external debt on its balance sheet, while the total cash on books at over $82M is at record highs and accounts for the largest chunk of the total asset base (almost 50%)!

This solid cash position is founded on an ever-improving ability to extract more free cash [FCF] from every dollar of sales incurred. Historically (over the last decade at least), INOD could only convert a little over 1% of its sales to FCF, but now, it is at impressive levels of over 15% (with the FCF having grown at a pace of 30% YoY in FY25)!

Given that INOD doesn't need to divert that free cash to paying down any debt, I don't see why INOD couldn't perhaps announce a new share buyback program (while it has authorization for a $2M share repurchase program, they have already fulfilled 90% of the authorization sum, with no repurchases having been carried out over the last couple of years) that could also help mitigate some of the dilution risks that have been growing of late (I touch upon this in the 'risks' section later on in the piece)

Valuation - Premium Multiple Reflects Strong Earnings Growth Potential

Passive observers of INOD who take a cursory glance at INOD's valuation grades on Seeking Alpha (a grade of C-) may not be overly enthused to pursue this stock. However, I believe some additional perspective may be required.

Prima facie, at 32x forward earnings, INOD isn't what one would necessarily term as cheap, more so when its data-refining peer set is priced at less than 15x earnings. However, do consider that it is now trading at a marginal discount to its long-term average of 35x (also, 6 months back, this business looked very pricey at over 80x forward earnings).

Crucially, those data refiners are only set to deliver long-term earnings growth of around 13% on average. INOD, on the other hand, justifies this premium valuation because its long-term earnings growth from last year's base of $0.919 through FY27 (consensus is budgeting for an EPS of $1.735) points to a business that is set to deliver a CAGR of 37%. This is not only 2.5x greater than the growth profile of its peers but also translates to a very compelling PEG (price-to-earnings growth) of less than 1x!

Technical Picture - Some Potential for Ongoing Momentum Loss to Abate

Those who've held INOD over the past year will be reasonably happy with its +20% expansion, but prospective investors should still be well aware that the stock hasn't quite been in vogue since mid-October 2025 and has been experiencing a loss of momentum. While there were some initial signs of stabilization at lower levels during November 2025 to January 2026, the price movement since has taken the form of lower lows [LLs] and lower highs [LHs]. In addition to that, INOD is now trading below all its key moving averages on the daily time frame, another validation of momentum loss.

Are there any silver linings as this momentum loss plays out? Yes, indeed.

For starters, do note that the short-sellers appear to be dialing down their enthusiasm (over the past month, the total shares sold short have declined by -6.5%).

Insider selling, which was rampant from November to January, appears to have dissipated in recent months.

Finally, switching over to the larger time frame weekly chart, we can see that last week, the stock tested an old price range (the $27-$35 levels) that had previously served as a source of support (during December 2024 to February 2025 and March 2025 to May 2025), and we could potentially see a repetition of the same. This thesis is supported by the fact that INOD has also been hugging its lower Bollinger band (which reflects overextension to the downside as it is two standard deviations lower than the 20-week moving average) for the last couple of weeks. Investors may also want to consider that the price drop in recent weeks has been accompanied by a relatively lower threshold of volumes, which potentially implies that bearish conviction isn't as resilient as before.

Risks

Although efforts are on to pursue new long-term customer relationships, at this juncture, INOD doesn't have the most diversified client base, with one Digital Data Solutions [DDS] customer in particular having a growing and pronounced impact on the former's total revenue (revenue contribution from this client expanded from 48% of group revenue in FY24 to 58% in FY25) and accounts receivable (this is instrumental for cash generation, as 63% of their accounts receivable comes from the same customer). If, for some reason, said client were to see a shift in strategic priorities, or resort to insourcing of data training, or choose to rejig its vendor base, it could end up hampering INOD's prospects in a big way.

Investors should also be mindful of potential dilution risk ahead, particularly as INOD is now stepping up the pace of its SBC (stock-based compensation), which surged by a whopping 179% last year! This could well be a lingering theme, as hiring AI-specialized data scientists just on a hefty base comp won't quite suffice.

Closing Thoughts

INOD, a data engineering specialist, is well poised to flourish in an era where agentic AI looks set to scale. INOD, which offers a stellar forward revenue and earnings growth profile, is not one of the cheaper data refiners around, but its valuations are now a tad lower than its long-term average. I also appreciate the company's balance sheet, which could support its growth ambitions, while its growing FCF prowess and lack of debt obligations could also stimulate share buyback momentum. Last week the stock also retested an old support and could potentially build a base there once again. To conclude, INOD is a buy.

data.title: CEF Insights: New Germany Fund For European Growth Opportunities
data.description:
CEFA:

Welcome to CEF Insights, your source for closed-end fund information and education, brought to you by the Closed-End Fund Association.

Today we are joined by Hansjoerg Pack, Director and Senior Portfolio Manager of Equities with DWS and Lead Portfolio Manager for the New Germany Fund, ticker symbol GF.

Hansjoerg, thank you for being with us today.

Hansjoerg Pack:

Thank you for having me.

CEFA:

Hansjoerg, I mentioned the New Germany Fund, a U.S.-listed closed-end fund you manage. Can you discuss the investment strategy as well as the objectives of the fund?

Hansjoerg Pack:

Sure, Diane. The fund seeks long-term capital appreciation, primarily through investments in middle-market German equities. Which means that a large proportion, at least 80% of net assets, ought to be invested in equity or equity-linked securities of German companies. And the focus is clearly on small and mid-sized companies.

CEFA:

Hansjoerg, over the past year, global markets have seen significant shifts in trade policy, supply chains, and energy policy. And Europe has dealt with the challenge of the war in Ukraine with Russia for over 4 years. However, German and European markets have been outperforming the US in recent months. Where do you see the German equity market currently?

Hansjoerg Pack:

Currently, we favor almost all other markets over the U.S. market. The main catalysts, I guess, were the election of Donald Trump in late 2024 and the announcement of the newly elected German government in early '25 to increase fiscal spending. The new US administration sends several messages across the Atlantic, which could not be mistaken. Germany announced a massive increase in fiscal spending, mainly on public infrastructure and defense, and laid out plans to reduce red tape in the administrative processes, for instance, adjusting required sustainability reporting for corporations. They were the main triggers.

CEFA:

Are you finding valuations to be attractive, and how do they compare to the U.S. equity market.

Hansjoerg Pack:

On valuations, let's not be misunderstood. We're not at the beginning of a major bull market, and equity valuations globally have gone a long way already, but not to extreme levels. What is interesting is that thanks to the perceived or real US exceptionalism and the run we have seen over the past years in the MAG 7 equities, the valuation premium that the US market always had over European markets has widened to an extreme level. At one point, it was greater than 50%.

CEFA:

What is your outlook for German equities as we move forward in 2026?

Hansjoerg Pack:

For 2026, we're quite constructive on German equities, supported by the fiscal impulse, political stability, and an improving growth momentum. In our house view, we see Germany as the main beneficiary of Europe's fiscal turn. We are expecting low double-digit earnings growth for the German market in 2026. And currently, we would prefer smaller and medium-sized companies in Europe and in Germany.

CEFA:

Hansjoerg, how do you see an allocation to an actively managed German equity strategy like New Germany Fund best positioned for a broadly diversified portfolio of a U.S. investor.

Hansjoerg Pack:

Look, the German economy roughly accounts for 4.5% of global GDP, according to the IMF or the World Bank data. On a PPP basis, it's probably a bit lower, say 4%. In terms of market cap, the German market only accounts for roughly 3% of global market cap.

CEFA:

Hansjoerg, thank you so much for taking the time to join us today.

Hansjoerg Pack:

My pleasure, Diane. Thank you.

CEFA:

We want to thank you for tuning into another CEF Insights podcast.

data.title: ISM Services PMI: Continued Expansion In March
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Originally published on April 6, 2026

The Institute for Supply Management (ISM) released its March Services Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI), with the headline composite index at 54.0. This was lower than the forecast of 54.8 and keeps the index in expansion territory for a 21st consecutive month.

Here is an excerpt from the report summary:

Miller continues, “March’s Services PMI® features the third month in a row with an increase in the 12-month PMI® average, up 0.6 percentage point from 51.7 percent in December 2025 to 52.3 percent. However, six of the 10 subindexes decreased month-over-month. The Prices Index increased, as expected, amid higher oil and fuel costs, and the Supplier Deliveries Index indicated slower performance compared to February, also unsurprisingly with shipping issues and flight disruptions due to the Middle East conflict and winter weather. Continuing strength in business activity, new orders and backlog of orders are positive economic signals, so the Employment Index dropping to its lowest level since December 2023 (43.5 percent) was a surprise.

“There are other signs of economic strength. Exports and imports activity have expanded for two months in a row for the first time since September and October 2024. The predominant commentary this month was about impacts and adjustments due to the conflict with Iran and the expected flow through of higher oil prices at some point. Companies across many industries reported seeing higher gas and diesel pricing, and inventories of multiple goods increased to withstand supply chain disruptions or short-term oil price impacts. Such construction products as lumber, copper and steel were noted as up in price. Although tariff impacts were still noted by panelists, Iran-related impacts dominated the comments in March.”

Here is a table showing the PMI's components.

Unlike its much older kin, the ISM manufacturing series, there is relatively little history for ISM's non-manufacturing data, especially for the headline composite index, which dates from 2008. The chart below shows the non-manufacturing composite.

The more interesting and useful sub-component is the non-manufacturing business activity index. In March, the index fell for the first time in six months, coming in at 53.9.

For a diffusion index, this can be an extremely volatile indicator, hence the addition of a six-month moving average to help us visualize the short-term trends.

Theoretically, this indicator should become more useful as the time frame of its coverage expands. Manufacturing may be a more sensitive barometer than non-manufacturing activity, but we are increasingly a services-oriented economy, which explains our intention to keep this series on the radar.

ISM PMI Comparison: Manufacturing vs. Services

The next chart offers a comparison between the two Purchasing Managers' Indexes provided by the Institute for Supply Management. See the latest update on the Manufacturing PMI here.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Archer Aviation: Buying The Dip Despite Manufacturing Challenges
data.description:
Being a growth investor is easier said than done. Especially if you have a tendency to invest in pre-revenue businesses with promising prospects. The biggest risk with these types of companies is that they might never reach the commercialization phase for their novel technology. When I first analyzed Archer Aviation, Inc. (ACHR) in November 2024, I immediately fell in love with the company as I thought its technological superiority within the eVTOL sector, partnerships with both commercial and government customers, and favorable regulatory support would help the company commercialize its technology. A few months after my original article, ACHR stock more than doubled, but only for most of these gains to be wiped out in the months that followed. As of this writing, ACHR stock is up 4.7% since my article, well below the S&P 500's 11% gain during the same period.

This underperformance has come on the back of massive shareholder ownership dilution (diluted shares outstanding have increased to 624 million from just 377 million in 2024), manufacturing delays, high cash burn, and failure to stick to the UAE launch timeline due to regional conflicts.

Today, I thought of revisiting my thesis to determine if this is a buy-the-dip opportunity with ACHR down 29% YTD. In this analysis, I will discuss the regulatory outlook for Archer, production challenges, and balance sheet health to evaluate its prospects. My findings lead me to believe that Archer is poised to lead the global eVTOL market in the long run.

The Regulatory Environment Is Supportive Of Commercialization

For any innovative technology, obtaining regulatory approval is the first hurdle. This is true for the eVTOL sector as well. Archer Aviation has materially de-risked itself from a regulatory perspective in the past couple of years both at the domestic level and the international level. This is one of the main reasons why I believe Archer is well-positioned for commercialization. Hold your horses before you jump to any conclusions here because achieving commercialization is one thing, but scaling production to reach profitability is another thing.

As revealed on the Q4 2025 earnings call, Archer has received the final acceptance of 100% of Midnight aircraft's Means of Compliance. This is a major de-risk event. This 100% acceptance of MOC confirms that Archer's Midnight testing strategy is on par with the FAA's expectations and that the eVTOL is ready for the final certification stage. For context, Joby Aviation (JOBY), Archer's arch-rival, has been stuck at a 97% MOC acceptance rate for a couple of years now. Archer will now move forward to TIA flight testing with the FAA. This is expected to begin this year.

This favorable development comes at a time when the U.S. has introduced a favorable framework to deploy eVTOLs domestically to dominate this market sector in the long run. The most significant program to note is the FAA's eVTOL Integration Pilot Program. Based on the official program document published by the FAA, this eIPP program is expected to run for 3 years after the first eVTOL project becomes operational.

Exhibit 1: eIPP objectives

Archer has made solid progress outside of the U.S. as well, mainly in the UAE. Interestingly, I call the UAE home (I am based in Dubai, as many of my close followers know).

In the UAE, the General Civil Aviation Authority is the regulatory authority for eVTOL. The UAE is arguably one of the biggest supporters of air taxi deployment. The GCAA has taken a very favorable stance on air taxis and the launch of eVTOL, and Archer has grabbed this opportunity with both hands. Archer is currently working with the Abu Dhabi Investment Office and the Abu Dhabi Airports Authority to obtain relevant regulatory approval to launch the first Midnight flight. Despite setbacks stemming from regional conflicts, Abu Dhabi (the capital of the UAE) is expected to see the first Midnight passenger flights.

Given this favorable regulatory outlook, Fortune Business Insights projects the eVTOL market to grow at a CAGR of 37% through 2034, lifting the market value to $17 billion from just $1 billion in 2025.

Scaling Production Is The Biggest Challenge

For Archer, I believe commercializing is about when, not if. Recent regulatory developments ensure that Archer will eventually commercialize its technology. But for a long-term-oriented investor, this is not a good enough reason to invest in the company just yet. We need to assess the production capabilities of Archer.

This is where things turn out to be challenging for the company.

Archer entered 2025 with an ambitious goal of producing 10 Midnight aircraft. This target was not met. By the end of the second quarter of 2025, Archer reported having 6 Midnight aircraft on the assembly line. Needless to say, the company is still in its infant stage when it comes to manufacturing eVTOL.

To address manufacturing challenges, Archer is utilizing an assembly line at its Silicon Valley facility with a focus on the final assembly operations for Midnight. Archer also opened a 400,000 square foot manufacturing facility in Georgia as well. According to company filings, this facility can handle up to 50 Midnight aircraft annually. The goal is to expand the manufacturing capacity of this facility to support up to 650 aircraft per year.

Archer's scaling challenges are universal. Commenting on the reasons why it is difficult to scale eVTOL production, CEO Adam Goldstein wrote:

Over the last seven years, what I have learned is that the hardest part of designing an eVTOL aircraft is perfecting the balance between (1) performance, (2) certification, and (3) mass manufacturability. You can design and build an eVTOL that performs beautifully. But try to certify it, and meeting the FAA's standards often requires you to add complexity and weight. That weight hurts performance and the complexity makes it harder to manufacture at scale. The design falls on its face. The key is solving all three simultaneously, and that is what we as an industry are all grappling with.

Although there is no denying that Archer, similar to other eVTOL players, will face scaling challenges, I believe it is better positioned than its rivals for a few reasons. The head start in regulatory approval is obviously one promising factor because it allows Archer to front-run the market. There are a few more reasons on top of this.

  1. Archer is partnering with Stellantis (STLA) to scale manufacturing. I believe this is a genius move given that Stellantis brings in decades of mass production experience. In fact, the Georgia plant was built using Stellantis' footprint.
  2. Archer sources the bulk of its supply (components and subsystems) from established aerospace suppliers such as Honeywell (HON) and Garmin (GRMN). I believe this gives Archer a meaningful competitive advantage over Joby as these suppliers have decades of experience in dealing with mass production.
  3. Archer is willing to sacrifice aircraft performance for ease of manufacturing. This is not to say Archer's aircraft are subpar quality. Archer, in fact, is leading the pack. The goal here is to focus on mass manufacturing capabilities rather than purely focusing on longer range and faster aircraft.

Overall, I believe Archer is well-positioned to mass-manufacture eVTOL ahead of its competitors. But investors may still have to weather major pains along the way when Archer moves into this phase of its business.

Risks

Archer Aviation is still a pre-revenue company. This makes it suitable only for long-term growth investors with an above-average risk appetite. Archer can still be displaced by a competitor. Despite progress on the regulatory approval front, Archer is far from mass manufacturing Midnight. This means that investors will have to patiently wait for meaningful revenue growth.

There is always the risk of ownership dilution as well. Archer ended 2025 with ~$2 billion in liquidity. Although this sounds promising, it's important to note that this liquidity has come on the back of significant shareholder ownership dilution. Shares outstanding have surged from 304 million in 2023 to 744 million as of the end of 2025 (including class B shares). In 2025 alone, Archer raised capital with 3 direct offerings. Archer used PIPE financing in 2024 in addition to ATM offerings. The 10-K also reveals that Archer has paid some of its vendors with equity as part of its efforts to preserve capital. Given this history, I believe investors should be ready for further ownership dilution in 2026 and beyond, at least until the company reaches commercialization.

Takeaway

Archer Aviation is making progress. The company has laid the groundwork to mass-produce eVTOL once regulatory clearance is sorted. There will be manufacturing challenges, but I feel comfortable exposing my portfolio to this risk and the ownership dilution risk given that I see a clear pathway for Archer to dominate the eVTOL market in the long run. If this happens, ACHR will handsomely reward long-term investors for bearing the short-term pain. As I expect the UAE launch to move forward eventually when geopolitical tensions subside, I have decided to add to my position in the coming weeks to bring my portfolio allocation to Archer to around 5%.

data.title: United Airlines: Iran Conflict Gift
data.description:
United Airlines Holdings, Inc. (UAL) got hit by the Iran conflict, and this was a gift for long-term investors in the airline. The stock is surging on a potential ceasefire and an end to the conflict, causing oil prices to collapse, but investors shouldn't trade United Airlines based on short-term hiccups in market dynamics. My investment thesis is ultra Bullish on the stock still trading below $100 despite strong earnings from a peer in the face of the saga in Iran.

Oil Impacts

Oil prices (CO1:COM) surged over $100 in the last month due to the conflict in Iran. United Airlines spent $11.4 billion on fuel costs last year, and oil is the second largest cost at nearly 20% of revenues in 2025.

A few weeks ago, CEO Scott Kirby warned about the impact of higher oil prices. In a letter to employees, the CEO warned of oil prices reaching $175 a barrel and staying at $100/bbl until 2027.

According to IATA data, jet fuel prices are at the highest levels in the last decade due to higher crack spreads than normal. United Airlines could definitely face up to $11 billion in additional fuel costs.

The airline made plans to cut up to 5 percentage points of capacity. The major worry with United Airlines is the company going too far to make adjustments in the face of normal oil prices. With signs of even a questionable ceasefire in Iran, WTI quickly plummeted back below $100.

Clearly, United Airlines prepared for unreasonably high oil prices. The big question when the company reports Q1 '26 earnings on April 21 is how these moves impact 2026 targets.

The airline plans to add 120 aircraft this year and another 130 new aircraft by April 2028. United Airlines will add 250 new aircraft during this period despite the worries about higher oil prices, so the company is still investing for the future despite the alarming commentary from the CEO.

Strong Demand

Prior to the market open, Delta Air Lines (DAL) led off the earnings season by reporting strong Q1 '26 earnings and guided to solid Q2 numbers. The legacy airline had already guided to solid Q1 numbers at the J.P. Morgan Industrials conference, so the real focus is on the positive indications for the June quarter despite high oil prices.

Delta Air Lines reported Q1 revenues grew nearly 13% and beat consensus analyst estimates by over $1 billion due in part to the numbers easily exceeding preliminary guidance. The ultimate key is the guidance for continued low-teens revenue growth for Q2 with flat capacity growth.

The airline is surviving and thriving despite both the TSA logjams delaying passengers and the Iran conflict pressuring expenses and causing the airlines to raise fares. Delta Air Lines still forecasts Q2 '26 EPS of $1.00 to $1.50 versus consensus at only $1.59, with the legacy airline taking a hit on a $2 billion increase in jet fuel expense.

United Airlines originally guided to 2026 EPS of $12 to $14. This EPS target is out of the window now, but investors should look at this original range as the normalized target going forward without a conflict.

The consensus analyst estimates have dropped to $9.92, and the Delta Air Lines numbers suggest Q2 estimates are slightly too high. The key is that investing should be based on the normalized 2027 EPS target of $14.54.

The stock has jumped back to nearly $100, but United Airlines still only trades at 6.6x EPS targets. The stock market or an individual investor has no way to predict how the Iran conflict plays out or where exactly oil prices trade in the short term, but the conflict is unlikely to last long term to where the Strait of Hormuz is closed for a lengthy period.

Naturally, the biggest risk is high prices remaining excessively high above $100 for an extended period. Delta Air Lines has already forecasted the ability for the legacy airline to remain profitable during this tough period, and the company still has the ability to hike fares and cut more capacity to ensure profits and cash flows remain positive.

The airlines have shown a long period now of overcoming higher oil prices. The risk is this outcome changing to where extended oil prices lower demand and prevent United Airlines from hiking fares. The stock could see a serious slump in that equation.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that United Airlines stock is too cheap now. The dip from the Iran conflict is a gift, with the stock trading at nearly $120 earlier in the year. Investors should use any weakness from a failed ceasefire to scoop up more shares.
